text:
Where does space begin? 🌌

Most people think getting to orbit just means flying really, really high. But altitude is the easy part.

Orbit is actually about going sideways. Fast. 🧵 👇
If you just fly straight up 400km and stop, you won't float in space. Earth's gravity will just pull you right back down to a fiery crash.
To 'stay' in space, a rocket has to turn horizontal and hit ~28,000 km/h. At that speed, you are falling, but you're going sideways so fast you constantly miss the ground.

"Floating" astronauts aren't weightless. They are just in continuous free-fall around the curve of the Earth. 🌍

We visualize these exact orbital mechanics in Section 2.9 of our curriculum. Come see the math in action: https://falakplatforms.co.uk/ 🔗

Everyone knows space is freezing cold. 🥶

So why is the ISS equipped with massive, ammonia-filled radiators just to keep it from overheating?

The counter-intuitive reality of thermal dynamics in a vacuum 🧵 👇

On Earth, if your computer gets hot, a fan blows cooler air over it (convection).

In space, there is no air. You can’t blow heat away. The *only* way to get rid of heat is to 'glow' it away as infrared light (radiation). And radiation is painfully slow.

So while space itself is cold, anything generating heat (computers, astronauts, batteries) in a vacuum will quickly cook itself to death if that heat isn't carefully radiated away.

Learn more about hostile environments in Section 1.3: https://falakplatforms.co.uk/ 🔗

type: Week 2 - The Tyranny of the Rocket Equation
text:
In aerospace, every gram matters. ⚖️

Unlike an airplane where most of the weight is the plane and cargo, a rocket is almost entirely composed of explosive fuel.

Welcome to the Tyranny of the Rocket Equation. 🧵 👇

Here is the brutal math of getting to Orbit:
To lift an object, you need fuel. But fuel is heavy. So you need MORE fuel to lift the fuel you just added.

This cycle spirals incredibly quickly.

The result? A typical rocket is ~90% fuel by weight.

The actual payload (the satellites or astronauts) usually makes up less than 2% of the rocket's total mass. You are essentially riding a barely-contained flying bomb.

Understanding this mass fraction is the first step to understanding space economy and launch costs.

Explore Section 2.5 of the FALAk open curriculum to dive deeper into the physics: https://falakplatforms.co.uk/ 🔗

Why do rockets always drop pieces of themselves on the way up? 🚀

It’s not just to look cool. It is absolute mathematical necessity due to the limits of chemical fuel.

Why "Staging" is the only way we get to orbit 🧵 👇

Remember the Rocket Equation? Carrying dead weight into space is a massive waste of energy.

As a rocket burns fuel, its massive fuel tanks empty out. Why spend precious fuel accelerating giant, empty metal tubes?

Instead, we drop them! By shedding the heavy engines and empty tanks of the 'first stage', the much lighter 'second stage' can accelerate much more easily into orbit.

Single-Stage-To-Orbit (SSTO) is the holy grail, but current fuel physics makes it nearly impossible.

Curious about the engineering behind these incredible separation mechanisms?

Check out Section 2.6 of our interactive curriculum: https://falakplatforms.co.uk/ 🔗

Not all rocket engines roar. Some barely whisper. 🔊

There are two main paradigms of moving around in space: Chemical Propulsion and Electric Propulsion (like Ion Thrusters).

Why do we use both? 🧵 👇

Chemical engines are the roaring beasts you see at launch. They mix fuel and oxidizer to create massive explosions.

Pros: Insane thrust. Gets you off the launchpad.
Cons: Terribly inefficient (Low Isp). Burns through fuel in minutes.

Electric Ion thrusters use magnetism to accelerate plasma.

Pros: Incredibly efficient (High Isp). Can thrust continuously for months.
Cons: Thrust is so weak it couldn't lift a piece of paper on Earth. It is strictly for deep-space cruising over long periods.

Mission design is about balancing what engines to use, and when.

Learn how engineers trade off thrust vs. efficiency in the FALAk propulsion modules: https://falakplatforms.co.uk/ 🔗

How do you move from one orbit to another without running out of fuel? ⛽

In space, you can't just steer like a car. You have to change your orbit by adjusting your speed.

The most elegant mathematical solution is the Hohmann Transfer. 🧵 👇

A Hohmann Transfer requires exactly two engine burns.

Burn 1: Fire engines to raise the highest point of your orbit (apogee) to intersect your target.
Burn 2: "Coast" until you hit that high point, then fire again to circularize.

It is the most fuel-efficient maneuver possible for orbital transfers.

The catch? It's slow. If you want to go faster to catch an intercept, you have to burn wildly inefficient amounts of fuel. In space, patience saves propellant.

Orbital maneuvers are beautiful displays of physics in motion.

We visualize and break down these orbital changes block-by-block at FALAk Space: https://falakplatforms.co.uk/ 🔗

Have you ever noticed how satellite photos of Earth always seem to have great lighting? 📸

That isn't a coincidence. They are placed in a very specific, deliberate orbit: the Sun-Synchronous Orbit (SSO).

Here’s how it works 🧵 👇

Most satellites orbit around the equator. But SSO satellites orbit nearly pole-to-pole.

However, they are angled *just right* so that as the Earth revolves around the Sun over the year, the satellite's orbital plane rotates slightly to match it.

The result? The satellite passes over any given spot on Earth at the exact same local solar time every day. The shadows in the photos are always the same angle, making it easy to compare changes over time.

It’s an incredible synergy of geometry and orbital mechanics designed specifically for Earth Observation.

Dive into the wild world of specialized orbits in Section 3 of the FALAk platform: https://falakplatforms.co.uk/ 🔗

✨ Unexplained Shocks Around a White Dwarf Star

How is RXJ0528+2838 creating such shock waves?  A recently discovered white dwarf star, the farther left of the two...

Space reveals something new every day. 🔗 https://apod.nasa.gov/apod/image/2602/WhiteDwarfShock_VLT_960.jpg

🔴 Launch Alert: Falcon 9 Block 5 | Starlink Group 6-103

Target: LEO
Liftoff: Space Launch Complex 40, Cape Canaveral SFS, FL, USA

A batch of 29 satellites for the Starlink mega-constellation - SpaceX's project...

🔗 https://spacelaunchnow.me/launch/falcon-9-block-5-starlink-group-6-103

❓ Why are rockets mostly fuel?

Fuel dominates mass. Payload is a tiny fraction. To lift more, you need more fuel, which adds weight, requiring more fuel. Rockets scale poorly.

This is part of FALAk's mission: making space education accessible to everyone.

#SpaceEducation
